text: The robotic system is used to perform a complete mesocolic excision during a right colectomy using a hanging technique. Port placement is visible in a straight line from the left upper quadrant to the right lower quadrant, spaced eight centimeters apart.

The transverse colon is sutured to the abdominal wall to free robotic instruments for dissection. The right colon is also sutured for suspension and exposure. The mesenteric envelope is opened, and tumors are dissected from a medial-lateral approach, exposing the duodenum. The ileocolic vessels are identified, with the ileocolic vein clipped and divided from the superior mesenteric vein. The distal ileum is identified, and the mesentery is divided to address adhesions in the pelvis, freeing the small intestine for later anastomosis.

With the robotic fourth arm providing exposure, the surgeon continues dissection cephalad, clipping and dividing the right colic vein and artery. The procedure is performed without laparoscopic assistance. The pancreas and duodenum are visible as dissection occurs over these structures. The suture is removed for further retraction, and the right branch of the midcolic vessels is clipped and divided.

The surgical team replaces a sponge underneath the transverse colon and pulls it caudally to enter the lesser sac. They divide the omentum and identify the right gastroepiploic vessels on the greater curvature of the stomach, which are divided. The dissection continues towards the hepatic flexure, connecting spaces around the gastrocolic trunk, and mobilizing the right colon using a previously removed suture to relieve tension.

The surgeon divides the distal ileum and marks it with a suture, then divides the transverse colon using a vessel sealer. All remaining attachments are severed, and the specimen is placed aside. The distal ileum is positioned close to the transverse colon, and the anastomosis is prepared in the right upper quadrant with a suture, using the fourth arm for exposure.

The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic anastomosis between the small intestine and transverse colon. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess the blood supply to both structures. A 45mm stapler is utilized for the anastomosis, with the fourth robotic arm aligning the tissues. The common channel opening is closed using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture in a canal suture technique.

The completed anastomosis is visually inspected, with the barbed suture locked in place. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess the blood supply to the anastomosis, confirming proper perfusion. The specimen is extracted through the stapler port, showing the complete mesocolic excision, including the omentum and mesentery attached to the right and transverse colon. The specimen is identified as a T3 N0 M0 well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor, with examination of thirty-three lymph nodes.
